subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! Open your eyes and look around you right now. What do you see? Perhaps the warm golden sunshine pouring through your window, the glow of a lamp, or the pages of this very lesson. You can see all of these things because of one amazing thing called light. In this unit you will become a real light explorer. You will discover where light comes from, why we cannot see in the dark, how shadows are made, and how a shiny mirror can bounce light back to your eyes.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: By the end of this unit, you will be able to reach these learning goals. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown as a scientist.
Name many light sources around you and say whether each one is natural or made by people.
Explain that we need light to see objects, and that in complete darkness we cannot see at all.
Describe what darkness is - it is not a thing by itself, it is simply what happens when light is missing or blocked.
Show how a shadow forms when an object blocks light, and explain what makes a shadow bigger, smaller, or point in a different direction.
Explain reflection - how light bounces off smooth, shiny surfaces such as mirrors and calm water.
Explain day and night as the result of the sun lighting one side of the spinning Earth.
Follow light safety rules, especially the golden rule of never looking straight at the sun.
Carry out simple, safe investigations about light using things from your own home.

Here are the important words for this unit. A good scientist learns to use the right words, just as a good carpenter learns the names of every tool. Read each word, its meaning, and the example. You will meet all of these words again inside the lesson.

concepts.title: What Is Light and Where Does It Come From?
concepts.explanation: Let us start with a big and simple idea. Light is a kind of energy that lets us see. Without light, the world would be completely dark and we would not be able to see anything at all - not our mothers, not our food, not even our own hands. Light is so ordinary that we often forget how special it is. But stop and think: every colour you have ever seen, every face you love, every beautiful sunset over the Indian Ocean, reached your eyes because of light. Some objects make their own light. We call these objects light…
concepts.example: Light sources can be sorted into two groups. Natural light sources are made by nature. The sun is a natural light source. So are the stars that sparkle over the desert at night, the bright flash of lightning during the…

concepts.title: Darkness Is the Absence of Light
concepts.explanation: Now let us think about the opposite of light: darkness. Many children believe that darkness is a thing that arrives, like a dark cloud that fills a room. But that is not true, and understanding the truth is an important science idea. Darkness is not a thing at all. Darkness is simply what we call a place where there is little or no light. When the light leaves, darkness is what is left behind. Think about a cupboard in your home. When the door is open and the room is bright, you can see the tins and bags inside.…
concepts.example: Darkness happens naturally too. At night, when your side of the Earth turns away from the sun, the sunlight can no longer reach you, and so it becomes dark. Deep inside a cave, where sunlight cannot travel, it is dark…

concepts.title: How We See Objects
concepts.explanation: Here is one of the most surprising ideas in this whole unit. How do your eyes actually see things? Many people think their eyes work like torches, sending out a beam that touches objects. That is not how seeing works. The truth is the other way around. Light from a source travels to an object, bounces off it, and then travels into your eyes. Your eyes catch the light, and that is how you see. Let us follow the path of light with an example. Imagine Amina is holding a bright red mango in the sunshine. First, light…
concepts.example: This explains something you already know from your own life. In a bright room you can see everything clearly. As the room gets dimmer, you can see less and less. In complete darkness you cannot see at all, no matter how…

concepts.title: Shadows and How They Form
concepts.explanation: Shadows are one of the most fun parts of studying light, and you can play with them almost anywhere. A shadow is a dark shape that appears when an object blocks the light. Remember that light travels in straight lines. When something solid stands in the way, the light cannot pass through it or bend around it. So behind the object there is a patch where the light did not reach, and that patch is the shadow. To make a shadow, you always need three things working together. First, you need a light source, such as the…
concepts.example: Not every material makes a strong shadow. Opaque materials, like wood, stone, metal, and your own body, block light completely and make dark, clear shadows. Transparent materials, like clean glass or clear water, let…

concepts.title: Reflection and Mirrors
concepts.explanation: You already learned that we see objects because light bounces off them into our eyes. When light bounces off a surface, we call it reflection. All objects reflect a little light, which is why we can see them. But some surfaces are especially good at reflecting light. Smooth, shiny surfaces like a mirror, a polished metal spoon, or the calm surface of water reflect light so well that they can show a clear picture, called an image. Why do mirrors work so well while a rough wall does not? The secret is smoothness.…
concepts.example: You can see reflection in nature too. When Hassan looks into a calm well or a still pond in the early morning, he can see the sky and his own face looking back at him. But if the wind blows and makes ripples on the…

concepts.title: Day and Night
concepts.explanation: Now let us put our light knowledge together to understand something that happens every single day of your life: day and night. During the day it is bright and we can see easily. During the night it is dark and we often need a lamp or torch. Why does this happen? It is all because of the sun and the spinning Earth. The Earth is shaped like a giant ball, and it is always slowly spinning, like a top. The sun can only light up one half of the ball at a time - the half that is facing it. When your part of the Earth is…
concepts.example: This is why the sun seems to rise in the east each morning and set in the west each evening. The sun is not really travelling across the sky. It is the Earth that is turning, carrying you around with it. When Khadija in…

explorations.title: The Light Source Hunt
explorations.context: To find and sort the light sources in your home into natural and artificial groups. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: Do you think you will find more natural light sources or more artificial ones inside your home? Write down or say your guess.
explorations.prompt: How many light sources did you find in total?
explorations.answer: How many light sources did you find in total? Did you find more natural or more artificial light sources? Was your prediction correct? Which light source do you think is the most useful in your home, and why? Was the sun on your list? Why is the sun the most important light source of all?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find and sort the light sources in your home into natural and artificial groups. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: Do you think you will find more natural light sources or more artificial ones inside your home? Write down or say your guess.

explorations.title: Growing and Shrinking Shadows
explorations.context: To find out what happens to a shadow when you move the light source closer to and farther from an object. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: When I move the torch closer to the object, will the shadow get bigger or smaller?
explorations.prompt: What happened to the shadow as the torch moved closer to the toy?
explorations.answer: What happened to the shadow as the torch moved closer to the toy? Was your prediction correct? Why do you think moving the light closer changes the shadow size? Only one thing was moved each time. Why is it important to change only one thing in an experiment?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out what happens to a shadow when you move the light source closer to and farther from an object. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: When I move the torch closer to the object, will the shadow get bigger or smaller?

explorations.title: The Bouncing Light Mirror Test
explorations.context: To discover which surfaces reflect light and pictures well, and which ones do not. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: Which surface will show your face most clearly - a mirror, a metal spoon, a bowl of water, or a piece of cloth?
explorations.prompt: Which surface gave the clearest reflection? Was your prediction correct?
explorations.answer: Which surface gave the clearest reflection? Was your prediction correct? What do the best reflectors have in common? Think about how smooth and shiny they are. What happened to the water reflection when you made the surface wobble? Why? Why can you not see your face in the cloth or dark paper?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To discover which surfaces reflect light and pictures well, and which ones do not. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: Which surface will show your face most clearly - a mirror, a metal spoon, a bowl of water, or a piece of cloth?

explorations.title: Day and Night with a Ball and a Torch
explorations.context: To show how the spinning Earth makes day and night, using a ball as the Earth and a torch as the sun. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: When the torch shines on one side of the ball, what will the other side be like?
explorations.prompt: When your sticker was on the lit side, was it day or night there?
explorations.answer: When your sticker was on the lit side, was it day or night there? What was happening on the other side of the ball at the same time? What made the day change into night for your sticker - the torch or the turning ball? Why can it be daytime for you and night-time for a child far across the world?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To show how the spinning Earth makes day and night, using a ball as the Earth and a torch as the sun. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: When the torch shines on one side of the ball, what will the other side be like?

methods.title: The Light Source Hunt
methods.example: To find and sort the light sources in your home into natural and artificial groups. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: Do you think you will find more natural light sources or more artificial ones inside your home? Write down or say your guess.
steps: Walk slowly through the rooms of your home with an adult.
Look for every object that makes its own light. Remember: a light source makes light, it does not just reflect it.
Each time you find one, write it down or tell your adult to write it.
Beside each one, mark N for natural or A for artificial.
Look out of a window too, and add any light sources you see outside, such as the sun.

methods.title: Growing and Shrinking Shadows
methods.example: To find out what happens to a shadow when you move the light source closer to and farther from an object. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: When I move the torch closer to the object, will the shadow get bigger or smaller?
steps: Stand the toy on a table a little way from the wall.
Shine the torch at the toy so its shadow appears on the wall.
Hold the torch far from the toy. Measure the shadow with the ruler and write down its length.
Now move the torch closer to the toy. Keep the toy and wall in the same place - only move the torch.
Measure the shadow again and write down its length.

methods.title: The Bouncing Light Mirror Test
methods.example: To discover which surfaces reflect light and pictures well, and which ones do not. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: Which surface will show your face most clearly - a mirror, a metal spoon, a bowl of water, or a piece of cloth?
steps: Place your objects on a table in a row.
Look into each one and try to see your face. Notice how clear the picture is each time.
Now shine the torch at each surface (never into your own eyes) and see how much light bounces back.
Give each surface a score from 1 (no reflection) to 5 (very clear reflection).
Gently touch the surface of the still water, then look again while it is wobbling. Notice the change.

methods.title: Day and Night with a Ball and a Torch
methods.example: To show how the spinning Earth makes day and night, using a ball as the Earth and a torch as the sun. Make a Prediction Before you start, guess: When the torch shines on one side of the ball, what will the other side be like?
steps: Stick a small sticker on the ball. This sticker is your home town, such as Hargeisa or Mombasa.
Ask an adult to hold the torch still. The torch is the sun.
Hold the ball in front of the torch so one side is lit and the other side is dark.
Notice: the lit side is having day, and the dark side is having night.
Slowly turn the ball around, like the spinning Earth.

workedExamples.title: Measuring How Shadows Change
workedExamples.prompt: Scientists like to measure things carefully so they can spot patterns. Let us work through an example together, step by step, so you can see how a scientist thinks. Imagine Farah does a shadow experiment with a small toy camel standing on the table and a…
workedExamples.solution: The question: What happens to the shadow when the torch moves closer to the toy camel? Step 1 - Set up carefully. Farah stands the toy camel on the table near a wall. He holds the torch far away at first and shines it at the camel so a shadow appears on the wall. Step 2 - Measure the first shadow. With the torch far away, Farah measures the shadow with a ruler. The shadow is 10 centimetres long. He writes this down. Writing down what you see is an important part of doing science. Step 3 - Change one thing. Farah…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 2
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these makes its OWN light? a) the moon b) a mirror c) a candle
workedExamples.solution: 2. c) a candle. A candle makes its own light with its flame. The moon and a mirror only reflect light.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 3
workedExamples.prompt: Darkness is best described as: a) a black thing that fills a room b) a place with little or no light c) a kind of cloud
workedExamples.solution: 3. b) a place with little or no light. Darkness is not a thing; it is what is left when light is missing.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 4
workedExamples.prompt: To see an object, light must: a) come out of your eyes b) bounce off the object into your eyes c) stay inside the object
workedExamples.solution: 4. b) bounce off the object into your eyes. Eyes do not send out light; they catch light that reflects off objects.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 5
workedExamples.prompt: To make a shadow you need a light, a surface, and: a) a mirror b) an opaque object c) water
workedExamples.solution: 5. b) an opaque object. An opaque object blocks the light, and the three things needed are a light, an opaque object, and a surface.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: A material that lets light pass straight through is called: a) opaque b) transparent c) shiny
workedExamples.solution: 6. b) transparent. Transparent materials, like clear glass and water, let light pass through.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: When you move a torch closer to an object, its shadow gets: a) bigger b) smaller c) disappears
workedExamples.solution: 7. a) bigger. Moving the light closer to the object makes the shadow grow larger.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Which surface reflects light the best? a) a smooth mirror b) a rough stone wall c) dark cloth
workedExamples.solution: 8. a) a smooth mirror. Smooth, shiny surfaces reflect light in a neat way and give a clear image.

practice.prompt: Which of these is a natural light source? a) a torch b) the sun c) a light bulb
practice.answer: 1. b) the sun. The sun is made by nature, so it is a natural light source. A torch and a bulb are made by people.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these makes its OWN light? a) the moon b) a mirror c) a candle
practice.answer: 2. c) a candle. A candle makes its own light with its flame. The moon and a mirror only reflect light.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Darkness is best described as: a) a black thing that fills a room b) a place with little or no light c) a kind of cloud
practice.answer: 3. b) a place with little or no light. Darkness is not a thing; it is what is left when light is missing.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To see an object, light must: a) come out of your eyes b) bounce off the object into your eyes c) stay inside the object
practice.answer: 4. b) bounce off the object into your eyes. Eyes do not send out light; they catch light that reflects off objects.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: To make a shadow you need a light, a surface, and: a) a mirror b) an opaque object c) water
practice.answer: 5. b) an opaque object. An opaque object blocks the light, and the three things needed are a light, an opaque object, and a surface.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A material that lets light pass straight through is called: a) opaque b) transparent c) shiny
practice.answer: 6. b) transparent. Transparent materials, like clear glass and water, let light pass through.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: When you move a torch closer to an object, its shadow gets: a) bigger b) smaller c) disappears
practice.answer: 7. a) bigger. Moving the light closer to the object makes the shadow grow larger.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which surface reflects light the best? a) a smooth mirror b) a rough stone wall c) dark cloth
practice.answer: 8. a) a smooth mirror. Smooth, shiny surfaces reflect light in a neat way and give a clear image.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: The moon shines because it: a) is on fire b) makes its own light c) reflects light from the sun
practice.answer: 9. c) reflects light from the sun. The moon makes no light of its own; it bounces sunlight back to us.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: We have day and night because: a) the sun switches off b) the Earth spins c) clouds cover the sun
practice.answer: 10. b) the Earth spins. As the Earth spins, each side takes turns facing the sun, giving day and night.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Your shadow outside is longest: a) at midday b) in the early morning and evening c) at night
practice.answer: 11. b) in the early morning and evening. When the sun is low, shadows stretch out long. At midday the sun is high and shadows are short.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is the safest rule when exploring light? a) look straight at the sun b) shine a torch in eyes c) never look straight at the sun
practice.answer: 12. c) never look straight at the sun. The sun's light is so strong it can damage your eyes, so this is the golden safety rule.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: The Light Source Hunt
activities.materials: A pencil and paper; Your own eyes; An adult to walk around with you
steps: Walk slowly through the rooms of your home with an adult.
Look for every object that makes its own light. Remember: a light source makes light, it does not just reflect it.
Each time you find one, write it down or tell your adult to write it.
Beside each one, mark N for natural or A for artificial.
Look out of a window too, and add any light sources you see outside, such as the sun.

activities.title: Growing and Shrinking Shadows
activities.materials: A torch or the torch on a phone (ask an adult); A small toy or a cup; A blank wall or a sheet of paper on the wall; A ruler; A darker room helps you see
steps: Stand the toy on a table a little way from the wall.
Shine the torch at the toy so its shadow appears on the wall.
Hold the torch far from the toy. Measure the shadow with the ruler and write down its length.
Now move the torch closer to the toy. Keep the toy and wall in the same place - only move the torch.
Measure the shadow again and write down its length.

activities.title: The Bouncing Light Mirror Test
activities.materials: A safe mirror (ask an adult); A shiny metal spoon; A bowl of still water; A piece of cloth or dark paper; A torch
steps: Place your objects on a table in a row.
Look into each one and try to see your face. Notice how clear the picture is each time.
Now shine the torch at each surface (never into your own eyes) and see how much light bounces back.
Give each surface a score from 1 (no reflection) to 5 (very clear reflection).
Gently touch the surface of the still water, then look again while it is wobbling. Notice the change.
Safety: Glass breaks — carry jars and mirrors with two hands, and tell an adult straight away if anything chips or cracks.

activities.title: Day and Night with a Ball and a Torch
activities.materials: A ball, an orange, or a round fruit; A torch; A small sticker or a dot drawn with pen; A darker room
steps: Stick a small sticker on the ball. This sticker is your home town, such as Hargeisa or Mombasa.
Ask an adult to hold the torch still. The torch is the sun.
Hold the ball in front of the torch so one side is lit and the other side is dark.
Notice: the lit side is having day, and the dark side is having night.
Slowly turn the ball around, like the spinning Earth.

activities.title: Observe: Reflection and Mirrors
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of reflection and mirrors around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Day and Night
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of day and night around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Light
Energy that lets us see. It travels very fast in straight lines.
Light source
Something that makes its own light, like the sun, a torch, or a candle.
Natural light
Light from nature: the sun, stars, lightning, and fire.
Artificial light
Light people make: bulbs, torches, and phone screens.
Darkness
What is left when there is little or no light. Not a thing by itself.
Shadow
A dark shape made when an opaque object blocks light.
Opaque
Blocks light completely, so it makes a clear shadow (wood, stone, your body).
Transparent
Lets light pass straight through (clear glass, clean water).
Reflection
When light bounces off a surface and changes direction.
Mirror
A smooth, shiny surface that reflects light very well.
Day
The bright time when your side of Earth faces the sun.
Night
The dark time when your side of Earth faces away from the sun.

vocabulary.meaning: Energy that lets us see. It travels very fast in straight lines.
vocabulary.example: You can see all of these things because of one amazing thing called light.

vocabulary.meaning: Something that makes its own light, like the sun, a torch, or a candle.
vocabulary.example: The sun is our biggest and most important light source.

vocabulary.meaning: Light from nature: the sun, stars, lightning, and fire.
vocabulary.example: The sun, the stars, lightning, and fire give natural light.

vocabulary.meaning: Light people make: bulbs, torches, and phone screens.
vocabulary.example: Artificial light sources are made by people.

vocabulary.meaning: What is left when there is little or no light. Not a thing by itself.
vocabulary.example: Explain that we need light to see objects, and that in complete darkness we cannot see at all.

vocabulary.meaning: A dark shape made when an opaque object blocks light.
vocabulary.example: Show how a shadow forms when an object blocks light, and explain what makes a shadow bigger, smaller, or point in a different direction.

vocabulary.meaning: Blocks light completely, so it makes a clear shadow (wood, stone, your body).
vocabulary.example: A wooden door and your own body are opaque, so they make shadows.

vocabulary.meaning: Lets light pass straight through (clear glass, clean water).
vocabulary.example: Clear glass and clean water are transparent - you can see through them.

vocabulary.meaning: When light bounces off a surface and changes direction.
vocabulary.example: Explain reflection - how light bounces off smooth, shiny surfaces such as mirrors and calm water.

vocabulary.meaning: A smooth, shiny surface that reflects light very well.
vocabulary.example: You will discover where light comes from, why we cannot see in the dark, how shadows are made, and how a shiny mirror can bounce light back to your eyes.

vocabulary.meaning: The bright time when your side of Earth faces the sun.
vocabulary.example: When you wake up on the Indian Ocean coast near Mombasa or Kismaayo, the bright morning sun tells you that a new day has begun.

vocabulary.meaning: The dark time when your side of Earth faces away from the sun.
vocabulary.example: When your family lights a torch or flashlight to walk safely across the compound at night, you are using light that people made.

commonMistakes: My eyes send out light to see.
Eyes only catch light. Light bounces off objects INTO your eyes.
Darkness is only the absence of light.
The moon makes its own light.
A shadow is a dark object.
A shadow is just blocked light on a surface.
The sun moves across the sky.
The Earth spins; the sun stays put.

connections.text: Earth and Space (next unit): Day, night, the sun, the moon, and its changing shapes all grow out of what you learned here.
Many artificial light sources, like bulbs and torches, work using electricity and batteries.
Plants need light to make their food, and animals like owls and bats are specially suited to the dark.
Measuring shadow lengths and giving reflections a score uses counting and comparing.
Light safety, using lamps at night, and finding shade all use this science.

fluency.answer: 1. b) the sun. The sun is made by nature, so it is a natural light source. A torch and a bulb are made by people.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

fluency.errorFeedback: 7. a) bigger. Moving the light closer to the object makes the shadow grow larger.

fluency.prompt: Which surface reflects light the best? a) a smooth mirror b) a rough stone wall c) dark cloth
fluency.answer: 8. a) a smooth mirror. Smooth, shiny surfaces reflect light in a neat way and give a clear image.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 8. a) a smooth mirror. Smooth, shiny surfaces reflect light in a neat way and give a clear image.

fluency.prompt: The moon shines because it: a) is on fire b) makes its own light c) reflects light from the sun
fluency.answer: 9. c) reflects light from the sun. The moon makes no light of its own; it bounces sunlight back to us.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 9. c) reflects light from the sun. The moon makes no light of its own; it bounces sunlight back to us.

fluency.prompt: We have day and night because: a) the sun switches off b) the Earth spins c) clouds cover the sun
fluency.answer: 10. b) the Earth spins. As the Earth spins, each side takes turns facing the sun, giving day and night.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 10. b) the Earth spins. As the Earth spins, each side takes turns facing the sun, giving day and night.

fluency.prompt: Your shadow outside is longest: a) at midday b) in the early morning and evening c) at night
fluency.answer: 11. b) in the early morning and evening. When the sun is low, shadows stretch out long. At midday the sun is high and shadows are short.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 11. b) in the early morning and evening. When the sun is low, shadows stretch out long. At midday the sun is high and shadows are short.

fluency.prompt: Which is the safest rule when exploring light? a) look straight at the sun b) shine a torch in eyes c) never look straight at the sun
fluency.answer: 12. c) never look straight at the sun. The sun's light is so strong it can damage your eyes, so this is the golden safety rule.
fluency.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.
fluency.errorFeedback: 12. c) never look straight at the sun. The sun's light is so strong it can damage your eyes, so this is the golden safety rule.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you know about light to answer these real-life questions.
realProblems.answer: 1. Safe choices include a battery torch and a solar lamp. A child can safely help with a battery torch. Candles and fire should only be handled by an adult.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1. Safe choices include a battery torch and a solar lamp. A child can safely help with a battery torch. Candles and fire should only be handled by an adult.

realProblems.prompt: The electricity goes out at night during a storm in Mogadishu. Name two safe light sources your family could use, and say which one a child could help with.
realProblems.answer: 2. She should take the goats under a tree, such as an acacia. It is cooler in the shadow because the tree blocks the sun's light, and light also carries warmth.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 2. She should take the goats under a tree, such as an acacia. It is cooler in the shadow because the tree blocks the sun's light, and light also carries warmth.

realProblems.prompt: Halima wants to rest her goats in the cool shade during the hot midday. Where should she take them, and why is it cooler in the shadow?
realProblems.answer: 3. He could use a mirror or another shiny object to flash sunlight to his friend. The light from the sun reflects (bounces) off the shiny surface toward his friend.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 3. He could use a mirror or another shiny object to flash sunlight to his friend. The light from the sun reflects (bounces) off the shiny surface toward his friend.

realProblems.prompt: Omar wants to signal to his friend far across a field using sunlight. What shiny object could he use, and what is happening to the light?
realProblems.answer: 4. The Earth is spinning. Nairobi is on the side facing the sun, so it is day there, while the other child is on the side turned away from the sun, so it is night for them.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 4. The Earth is spinning. Nairobi is on the side facing the sun, so it is day there, while the other child is on the side turned away from the sun, so it is night for them.

realProblems.prompt: It is bright morning in Nairobi but a child in another country is asleep in the dark. Explain how this can be true at the same time.
realProblems.answer: 5. She should sit so the lamp shines on her book, not into her eyes. Light needs to fall on the page and bounce into her eyes so she can see the words clearly and comfortably.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 5. She should sit so the lamp shines on her book, not into her eyes. Light needs to fall on the page and bounce into her eyes so she can see the words clearly and comfortably.

realProblems.prompt: Zahra wants to read comfortably in the evening. Should she sit so the lamp shines on her book or into her eyes? Explain your choice.
realProblems.answer: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Work through the task and check with your teacher or the reference card.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name two natural light sources and two artificial light sources.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1. Natural: the sun and the stars (or lightning, or fire). Artificial: a torch and a light bulb (or a candle, or a phone screen).

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain in your own words what darkness is.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 2. Darkness is what happens when there is little or no light. It is not a thing by itself - it is simply light being missing or blocked.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Describe the path light takes for you to see a red ball in the sunshine.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 3. Light leaves the sun, travels to the red ball, bounces off the ball, and travels into your eyes. Then you see the ball.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What are the three things you need to make a shadow?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 4. A light source, an opaque object to block the light, and a surface for the shadow to fall on.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does a mirror show a clear picture but a rough wall does not?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 5. A mirror is smooth, so light bounces off in a neat, orderly way and the picture stays together. A rough wall scatters the light in all directions, so no clear picture forms.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why is the moon NOT a light source?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 6. The moon makes no light of its own. It only reflects sunlight back to us, so it is a reflector, not a light source.

questions.question: Which of these is a natural light source?

questions.explanation: the sun. The sun is made by nature, so it is a natural light source. A torch and a bulb are made by people.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a candle. A candle makes its own light with its flame. The moon and a mirror only reflect light.

options: a black thing that fills a room

questions.explanation: a place with little or no light. Darkness is not a thing; it is what is left when light is missing.

questions.hint: Use the Unit 4 Science Words reference.

selfAssessment: I can by the end of this unit, you will be able to reach these learning goals. Read them now, and read them again at the end to see how much you have grown as a scientist.
I can name many light sources around you and say whether each one is natural or made by people.
I can explain that we need light to see objects, and that in complete darkness we cannot see at all.
I can describe what darkness is - it is not a thing by itself, it is simply what happens when light is missing or blocked.
I can show how a shadow forms when an object blocks light, and explain what makes a shadow bigger, smaller, or point in a different direction.
I can explain reflection - how light bounces off smooth, shiny surfaces such as mirrors and calm water.
I can explain day and night as the result of the sun lighting one side of the spinning Earth.
I can follow light safety rules, especially the golden rule of never looking straight at the sun.
